coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot-based type I photosensitizers for photocatalytic oxidation reaction of arylboric acid and N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinoline
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Zhong-Lin
Niu, Kai-kai
Lv, Yu-Guang
Xing, Ling-Bao
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising materials for photocatalytic organic transformations due to their excellent photostability, tunable electronic properties, and environmental friendliness; However, the ability of CDs to selectively generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and its integration with organic photocatalytic synthesis applications has always been a long-term challenge. In this work, we synthesized a new nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) with enhanced light absorption and notable efficiency in generating superoxide anion (O2 •−) selectively. Leveraging the selective generation of superoxide anions, we achieved highly efficient photooxidation of boronic acids and N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinolines, demonstrating the practical applicability of N,P-CDs as photocatalysts and represents good functional-group tolerance as well as a broad substrate scope. This study provides valuable insights into the design of carbon-based photocatalysts with controlled ROS generation, opening new avenues for environmentally benign organic transformations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N,P-doped carbon dots
Superoxide anion
Photooxidation
Photocatalysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM images; (b) Particle size; (c) Zeta potential; (d) UV–vis absorption spectra; (e) fluorescence emission spectra (λex = 225 nm); (f) schematic representation of the proposed mechanism for photosensitization of N,P-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of ABDA with N,P-CDs after light irradiation; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of TMPD with N,P-CDs after light irradiation; (c) Singlet oxygen detection in the TEMPO-based EPR spectroscopy; (d) Superoxide anion detection in the DMPO-based EPR spectroscopy.

(a) Controlled experiments of photooxidation of pyridine-4-boronic acid with different scavengers, BQ for O2 •−, NaN3 for 1O2, TEA for •OH and KI for hole; (b) Controlled experiments of photooxidation of N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinoline with different scavengers.
(a) Proposed mechanism of photooxidation toward boronic acid; (b) Proposed mechanism of photooxidation toward N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinoline.
The design and synthesis of N,P-CDs.

Optimization of reaction conditions for the oxidation of boronic acids.
a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions: Pyridine-4-boronic acid (24.5 mg, 0.2 mmol), N,P-CDs (1.0 mg), DIPEA (69.7 μL, 0.4 mmol), MeCN (2.0 mL), 395 nm LED (10 W), r.t., air, 24 h.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
The scope of the substrate with regard to the various boronic acids.
a
,
b

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions: Boronic acid (0.2 mmol), NP-CDs as photocatalysts (1.0 mg), DIPEA (69.7 μL, 0.4 mmol), MeCN (2.0 mL), 395 nm LED (10 W), air, r.t., 24 h.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Optimization of reaction conditions for the oxidation of N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinolines.
a

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions: N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinoline (41.8 mg, 0.2 mmol), N,P-CDs (1.0 mg), MeCN (2.0 mL), 395 nm LED (10 W), air, r.t., 24 h.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Substrate scope with respect to N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives.
a
,
b

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Reaction conditions: N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives (0.2 mmol), NCDs (1.0 mg), MeCN (2.0 mL), 395 nm LED (10 W), air, r.t., 24 h.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot-based type I photosensitizers for photocatalytic oxidation reaction of arylboric acid and N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinoline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiamusi University, Jiamusi 154007, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Materials Science and Engineering, Jiamusi University, Jiamusi 154007, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiamusi University, Jiamusi 154007, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bSchool of Pharmacy, Jiamusi University, Jiamusi 154007, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cSchool of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shandong University of Technology, Zibo 255000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as promising materials for photocatalytic organic transformations due to their excellent photostability, tunable electronic properties, and environmental friendliness; However, the ability of CDs to selectively generate reactive oxygen species (ROS) and its integration with organic photocatalytic synthesis applications has always been a long-term challenge. In this work, we synthesized a new nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) with enhanced light absorption and notable efficiency in generating superoxide anion (O2 •−) selectively. Leveraging the selective generation of superoxide anions, we achieved highly efficient photooxidation of boronic acids and N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinolines, demonstrating the practical applicability of N,P-CDs as photocatalysts and represents good functional-group tolerance as well as a broad substrate scope. This study provides valuable insights into the design of carbon-based photocatalysts with controlled ROS generation, opening new avenues for environmentally benign organic transformations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic photochemistry is undergoing rapid advancement, marking a pivotal area of growth in the broader field of organic chemistry [1-4]. This discipline focuses on examining atomic and molecular structures when they enter electronically excited states, alongside the analysis of their associated physical and chemical behaviors [5]. Photochemical reactions employ light as a stimulus to explore the dynamics of molecules in excited states [6-18]. This synthetic strategy is designed with the goals of maximizing cleanliness, optimizing energy use, and promoting resource conservation [19-25]. These advancements introduce cutting-edge methods, innovative strategies, and emerging technologies to organic synthesis, thereby providing new insights into the evolution of modern synthetic chemistry [26-30]. Consequently, the creation of innovative, highly efficient photocatalysts capable of driving chemical transformations in environmentally benign solvents is of critical significance.

Carbon dots (CDs) represent an emerging class of carbon-based nanomaterials with zero-dimensional, typically defined by their 10 nm dimensions [31-35]. Due to their remarkable luminescence, broad availability, and excellent biocompatibility, CDs have been widely employed in various fields, including biosensing, bioimaging, sensor technology, nanomedicine, optoelectronics, drug delivery, and catalysis [36-54]. In particular, CDs are ideal candidates for catalytic applications in photocatalytic systems due to their ability to efficiently tune fluorescence emission wavelengths, coupled with their high fluorescence quantum yield. These properties have led to their widespread use in pioneering chemical processes, including artificial photosynthesis and various organic transformations. Reactive surface functionalities on light-absorbing CDs have opened up new avenues for catalytic applications, facilitating the synthesis of valuable organic compounds via light-induced radical processes under relatively mild conditions [55-59]. Consequently, there has been an increasing interest in the preparation of CDs and the integration of them into organic photocatalysis in the past few years, especially due to their environmentally friendly characteristics and lack of metals, which position them as promising nanophotocatalysts for a variety of photocatalytic transformations [60-64]. For instance, Kang et al. described the modification of CDs to add external surface hydroxyl groups via electrochemical etching, which were successfully employed as efficient heterogeneous nanocatalysts for H-bonding catalysis in aldol condensation reactions [65]. Similarly, Similarly, Wu et al. employed readily manufactured, highly fluorescent crystalline CDs to establish an extremely effective borylation approach for diazonium compounds in water circumstances, which displayed excellent tolerability and efficiency [66]. However, a key challenge remains in identifying and controlling the photoactive species within CDs during photocatalytic processes, as well as directing their application toward specific photocatalytic reactions, which warrants further investigation.

Nitrogen and Phosphorus co-doped carbon dots (N,P-CDs) were efficiently synthesized via a standard one-step hydrothermal approach, employing ethylenebis (nitrilodimethylene) tetraphosphonic acid as the carbon precursor and ethylenediamine as the cross-linking agent. The resulting N,P-CDs were characterized using a range of techniques, including high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) and steady-state spectroscopic analyses. TEM images and particle size analysis reveal that N,P-CDs possess a uniform spherical morphology with excellent dispersibility, and the individual particle size is approximately 3–4 nm (Fig. 1 a and Fig. 1b). Additionally, zeta potential analysis serves as an essential method for assessing the surface charge characteristics of N,P-CDs (Fig. 1c). And the results reveal that the N,P-CDs displayed a negative potential of −7.85 mV. In parallel, the UV–vis absorption displayed two prominent absorption peaks at 214 nm and 334 nm, corresponding to the π → π* and n → π* transitions. Simultaneously, the fluorescence emission spectra of the N,P-CDs exhibited a peak emission at 450 nm, displaying a distinctive blue fluorescence.

Given the photophysical parameters exhibited by N,P-CDs, it is postulated that these materials are prone to undergo photoexcitation, resulting in an excited state that subsequently undergoes intersystem crossing (ISC), and subsequently facilitating electron transfer with oxygen to form superoxide anions (Fig. 1f). To investigate this, we conducted tests on reactive oxygen species under photocatalytic conditions. 9,10-Anthracenediyl-bis(methylene)dimalonic acid (ABDA) was employed as a probe for singlet oxygen, while N,N,N',N'-Tetramethyl-p-phenylenediamine (TMPD) was used to detect superoxide anions. As shown in the Fig. 2 a, the UV absorption spectrum of ABDA shows only a slight decrease, indicating that the generation of singlet oxygen under light irradiation (395 nm) is minimal. In contrast, the UV absorption peak of TMPD exhibits a significant increase, suggesting the generation of a large amount of superoxide anions (Fig. 2b). This result was further corroborated by Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) experiments. As illustrated in Fig. 2c, no EPR signal was observed when TEMPO was used as 1O2 probe, either before or after light irradiation. In contrast, a pronounced EPR signal was detected when DMPO was used as the probe for O2 •−after light irradiation (Fig. 2d). This indicates that the catalyst selectively generates superoxide anions. Building on the excellent ability of N,P-CDs to selectively generate O2 •− under photocatalytic conditions, we subsequently explored the catalytic system's capacity for boronic acid degradation and the oxidation of N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinoline.

We first optimized the reaction conditions for boronic acid degradation, as shown in Table 1 . Using 4-pyridylboronic acid (1a, 0.2 mmol) as the model substrate, the reaction was conducted with 1.0 mg of N,P-CDs and 2 equiv. of DIPEA in acetonitrile at room temperature for 24 h, yielding the target product 2a with 99 % yield (entry 1). When the catalyst loading was reduced to 0.5 mg, a significant drop in yield was observed (entry 2). Conversely, increasing the catalyst amount to 2.0 mg resulted in no noticeable improvement, with the yield remaining consistent with that of the 1.0 mg condition (entry 3). Additionally, shortening the reaction time led to a marked decrease in yield (entry 4). Solvent screening indicated that acetonitrile was the optimal solvent, outperforming water, DCM, DMF, and THF (entries 5–8). Finally, control experiments demonstrated that the catalyst, light source, base, and air are all crucial for the process (entries 9–12).

The substrate scope for the oxidative degradation of boronic acids was investigated under optimized reaction conditions, with the results presented in Table 2 . In addition to compatibility with pyridyl groups, the reaction also tolerates simple aromatic rings such as toluene (95 % for 2b). A range of toluenes substituted with electron-donating groups, including methyl (97 % for 2c), tert‑butyl (93 % for 2d), methoxy (90 % for 2e), and amino groups 89 % for 2f), afforded the desired products with excellent yields. Moreover, the reaction is compatible with aryl boronic acids bearing halogen substituents such as fluoro (87 % for 2 g), chloro (90 % for 2 h), and bromo (85 % for 2i) groups. The reaction also demonstrated tolerance for electron-withdrawing groups, including nitro (80 % for 2j), aldehyde (76 % for 2k, 83 % for 2l), and cyano functionalities (74 % for 2 m, 76 % for 2n). Lastly, pyrimidyl boronic acid was found to be a compatible substrate as well (82 % for 2o).

Subsequently, we optimized the reaction conditions for the photochemical oxidative conversion of N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinoline (Table 3 ). Employing N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinoline (3a, 0.2 mmol) as a model substrate, and using 1.0 mg of N,P-CDs as the catalyst in acetonitrile at ambient temperature for 24 h, the target product 4a was achieved with an impressive 95 % yield (entry 1). In contrast to boronic acid degradation, this transformation did not necessitate the inclusion of DIPEA as a base. Reducing the catalyst loading to 0.5 mg resulted in a marked decrease in yield (entry 2). Conversely, increasing the catalyst amount to 2.0 mg did not lead to any significant enhancement in yield (entry 3). Furthermore, shortening the reaction duration caused a considerable decline (entry 4). A comparative analysis of the efficacy of various solvents indicated that acetonitrile exhibited superior performance, surpassing water, DCM, DMF, and THF (entries 5–8). Control experiments ultimately confirmed that the presence of the catalyst, light source, base, and air were all indispensable for the efficient advancement of the reaction (entries 9–11).

To evaluate the feasibility of N,P-CDs toward different N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinoline derivatives, a range of substrates were tested in the photooxidation reactions (Table 4 ). A series of quinolinone derivatives with fluoro (89 % for 4b) and chloro (90 % for 4c) halogen substitutions, as well as methyl (94 % for 4d), methoxy (98 % for 4e), and nitro-substituted phenyl rings (80 % for 4f), were found to be moderately effective substrates.

After exploring the substrate scope, we proceeded to investigate the reaction mechanism. In the standard boronic acid degradation system, we introduced four inhibitors including 1,4-Benzoquinone (BQ), sodium azide (NaN3), triethylamine (TEA), and potassium iodide (KI) with the objective of facilitating the trapping of photogenerated 1O2, O2 •−, ·OH and holes (h⁺). Under standard reaction conditions, we observed that only BQ and KI exhibited significant inhibitory effects, indicating that both play critical roles in the reaction process (Fig. 3 a). The results from the O2 •− inhibitor align with our earlier findings. Similarly, for the oxidation of N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinoline, the inhibitory effects of these four inhibitors mirrored those observed in boronic acid degradation (Fig. 3b). Notably, BQ completely suppressed the reaction, further suggesting that O2 •− and holes are essential for the oxidation of N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinoline.

Based on these control experiments and relevant literature, we propose the following reaction mechanism in Fig. 4 . Upon light excitation, the carbon dots generate electron-hole pairs. Oxygen is reduced by the electrons to form superoxide anions. In the boronic acid oxidation reaction (Fig. 4a), the initial step involved the oxidation of DIPEA by the hole, resulting in the formation of a nitrogen-centered radical cation intermediate, I. The superoxide anion then reacts with boronic acid to form a boronic acid-superoxide radical intermediate II. This intermediate undergoes hydrogen atom transfer (HAT) with I, yielding a hydroperoxide intermediate III, which is unstable and undergoes intramolecular rearrangement to form a boronate ester intermediate IV. Subsequent hydrolysis leads to intermediate V alongwith the loss of a boronic acid molecule, producing the desired product. With regard to the oxidation of N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinoline (Fig. 4b), the substrate was initialised by the hole to generate a nitrogen radical cation VI. This intermediate undergoes hydrogen atom transfer with the superoxide anion, yielding a nitrogen-centered radical VII and a hydroxylperoxy radical. Radical VII then couples with the hydroxylperoxy radical to form a hydroperoxide intermediate VIII, which dehydrates to give the final product.

In conclusion, this study has demonstrated both the preparation and synthesis application of N,P-CDs as highly effective photocatalysts for organic transformations, offering enhanced light absorption and selective O2 •− generation. N,P-CDs had demonstrated remarkable photocatalytic activity in the selective oxidation of boronic acids and N-phenyl tetrahydroisoquinolines, exhibiting broad substrate scope, high yields and the use of air as a cost-effective and environmentally be nign oxidant. Their ability to control ROS generation, particularly O2 •− had allowed for efficient and targeted organic transformations under mild conditions. These findings had underscored the potential of N,P-CDs as eco-friendly, highly selective and metal-free photocatalysts, contributing to the design of sustainable systems for diverse organic reactions.

Preparation of N,P-CDs: The catalyst CDs were fabricated via solvothermal procedure conducted in H2O. A solution was prepared by dissolving of ethylenebis (nitrilodimethylene) tetraphosphonic acid (1.7 g, 4.0 mmol) and of ethylenediamine (1.1 mL, 16 mmol) in 20 mL of ultrapure water. The sample was then transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h After completed, solid impurities were removed by centrifugation. Subsequently, the sample was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane in order to facilitate further purification. The filtrate was subsequently purified by dialysis for 24 h, with the objective of eliminating residual compounds of low molecular weight. Ultimately, the dialyzed liquid was lyophilized, thereby yielding the N,P-CDs for future applications.

Zhong-Lin Guo: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kai-kai Niu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yu-Guang Lv: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Ling-Bao Xing: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2020MB018).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.mcat.2024.114625.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Merging photoredox catalysis with organocatalysis: the direct asymmetric alkylation of aldehydes
